lessons.subject: rla
lessons.title: Finding the Central Idea in Informational Text
lessons.intro: Informational text teaches you true facts about the real world. In this lesson you will spot what makes a text informational and find its central idea—the one big point the whole text is about.

vocabulary.meaning: Writing that gives true facts about real topics, like animals, places, or how things work.
The most important point the whole text is trying to tell you.
The one thing the text is mostly about.

body.text: What makes a text informational?
Informational text gives you real facts. It often has a title and headings, and it may use pictures, captions, bold words, or charts to help explain the topic.

items: It tells true facts, not a made-up story.
It has a clear topic, like "honeybees" or "volcanoes".
It often uses headings, bold words, or pictures with labels.

body.text: Finding the central idea
First find the topic by asking, "What is this mostly about?" Then ask, "What is the most important thing the author wants me to know about that topic?" That answer is the central idea.

body.title: Remember
body.text: The title and the first sentence often point you toward the central idea.

body.alt: A diagram showing three details supporting one central idea at the top
body.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 300 140' role='img'><rect x='75' y='8' width='150' height='34' rx='6' fill='#cfe8ff' stroke='#2563eb'/><text x='150' y='30' font-size='12' text-anchor='middle' fill='#1e3a8a'>Central Idea</text><rect x='10' y='95' width='80' height='34' rx='6' fill='#e5e7eb' stroke='#6b7280'/><text x='50' y='116' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Detail</text><rect x='110' y='95' width='80' height='34' rx='6' fill='#e5e7eb' stroke='#6b7280'/><text x='150' y='116' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Detail</text><rect x='210' y='95' width='80' height='34' rx='6' fill='#e5e7eb' stroke='#6b7280'/><text x='250' y='116' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Detail</text><line x1='150' y1='42' x2='50' y2='95' stroke='#6b7280'/><line x1='150' y1='42' x2='150' y2='95' stroke='#6b7280'/><line x1='150' y1='42' x2='250' y2='95' stroke='#6b7280'/></svg>

prompt: A text has the title "How Bees Make Honey" with headings, a labeled picture of a hive, and only true facts. What kind of text is this?

options.text: A made-up story about a talking bee

options.rationale: It gives true facts and uses headings and a labeled picture, which are features of informational text.

answer: B. Informational text
explanation: The true facts, title, headings, and labeled picture are all clues that this is informational text.

prompt: A text explains that bees collect nectar, store it in the hive, and fan it with their wings until it turns into honey. What is the central idea?

options.rationale: This is only one small detail, not the big point.

options.text: Bees follow several steps to make honey
options.rationale: This is the most important point that ties all the details together.

answer: B. Bees follow several steps to make honey
explanation: All the details describe steps bees take, so the central idea is that bees make honey through several steps.

lessons.subject: rla
lessons.title: Author's Purpose and Message
lessons.intro: Every author writes for a reason. In this lesson you will figure out why an author wrote a text and what message they want you to remember.

vocabulary.meaning: The reason an author writes: to persuade, to inform, or to entertain.
The big idea or lesson the author wants you to take away.
To try to make readers think or do something.

body.text: Why do authors write?
Authors usually write for one of three reasons. A helpful way to remember them is the letters P, I, and E.

body.html:
PurposeWhat it meansClue words
PersuadeConvince you to agree or actshould, best, must
InformTeach you factsfacts, steps, because
EntertainTell a fun storycharacters, once, suddenly

body.text: Finding the message
The message is what the author wants you to feel or learn. Ask yourself, "What does the author want me to understand after reading this?"

body.title: Tip
body.text: Look at the title and ending. Authors often hint at their message there.

prompt: An author writes, "You should always wear a helmet. It is the best way to keep your head safe." What is the author's purpose?

options.text: To entertain with a funny story
To persuade readers to wear a helmet
options.rationale: Words like "should" and "best" show the author is trying to convince you.

options.text: To explain how a helmet is made

answer: B. To persuade readers to wear a helmet
explanation: The words "should" and "best" are clues that the author wants to convince readers to do something.

prompt: A story ends with a boy sharing his lunch and feeling happy that he helped a friend. What message is the author sharing?

options.text: Helping others can make you feel good
options.rationale: The ending shows the boy feeling happy because he helped, which points to the message.

answer: B. Helping others can make you feel good
explanation: The boy feels happy after helping, so the author's message is that helping others feels good.

lessons.subject: rla
lessons.title: Making Inferences with Text Evidence
lessons.intro: Sometimes an author does not tell you everything. You become a reading detective and figure it out. In this lesson you will make inferences and back them up with evidence from the text.

vocabulary.meaning: A smart guess you make using text clues plus what you already know.
Words or sentences from the text that prove your idea.
A detail the author gives that helps you figure something out.

body.text: What is an inference?
An inference is a guess that is backed up by proof. The author gives you clues, you mix them with what you already know, and you figure out something the author did not say directly.
How to make an inference

items: Find a clue in the text.
Think about what you already know.
Put them together to make a guess.
Point to the words that prove it.

body.title: Remember
body.text: A good inference always has evidence from the text behind it.

prompt: The text says, "Maria grabbed her umbrella and pulled on her boots before opening the door." What can you infer?

options.text: It is sunny and warm outside
options.rationale: Umbrellas and boots are not needed in sunny weather.
Grabbing an umbrella and boots are clues that point to rain.

answer: B. It is probably raining outside
explanation: The umbrella and boots are clues. People use them in the rain, so you can infer it is raining.

prompt: The text says, "Sam's stomach growled loudly and he kept staring at the clock until lunchtime." Which detail is the BEST evidence that Sam is hungry?

options.rationale: A growling stomach is a direct clue that a person is hungry.

answer: A. Sam's stomach growled loudly
explanation: A growling stomach is the strongest clue that Sam is hungry, so it is the best evidence.

lessons.subject: rla
lessons.title: Using Context Clues to Find Word Meaning
lessons.intro: When you meet a word you do not know, you do not always need a dictionary. The other words in the sentence can help you. In this lesson you will use context clues to figure out what a word means.

vocabulary.meaning: The other words and sentences around a word.
A hint in the sentence that helps you guess a word's meaning.
When the sentence explains the word right there.

body.text: Words around the word
When you read a hard word, do not skip it. Read the rest of the sentence and look for clues that tell you what it means.
Kinds of context clues

items: Definition: the meaning is given right in the sentence.
Example: the sentence gives examples of the word.
Synonym: a word nearby means almost the same thing.
Antonym: a word nearby means the opposite.

body.html: Example: "The path was narrow, so thin that only one person could walk on it." The words "so thin" tell you narrow means not wide.

body.title: Tip
body.text: Try reading the sentence with your guess instead of the hard word. If it makes sense, you found the meaning.

prompt: Read the sentence: "The room was so cluttered, full of toys and clothes everywhere, that we could not find the floor." What does cluttered mean?

options.rationale: This is the opposite of the clues in the sentence.
The words "full of toys and clothes everywhere" tell you the meaning.

answer: B. Messy and full of things
explanation: The clue "full of toys and clothes everywhere" shows that cluttered means messy and full.

prompt: Read the sentence: "Unlike his shy sister, Ben was bold and loved to speak in front of the class." What does bold mean?

options.rationale: The word "Unlike" shows bold is the opposite of shy, so it means brave.

answer: A. Brave and confident
explanation: The word "Unlike" tells you bold is the opposite of shy, so bold means brave and confident.

lessons.subject: rla
lessons.title: Using Text Evidence to Support Your Answer
lessons.intro: When you answer a question about a text, you need proof. In this lesson you will learn to find and use text evidence so your answers are strong and correct.

vocabulary.meaning: Exact words or details from the text that prove your answer.
To back up your answer with proof from the text.
To point to or quote the part of the text that proves your idea.

body.text: Why use evidence?
Your answer is only strong if you can prove it. Text evidence shows where in the text you found your answer.
Steps to use evidence

items: Read the question carefully.
Go back into the text to find proof.
Choose the detail that best matches the question.
Use that detail in your answer.

body.title: Sentence starter
body.text: You can begin your answer with: "The text says..." or "I know this because..."

prompt: Question: Why was Theo upset? The text says, "Theo's kite was stuck high in the tree, and he could not reach it." Which sentence best supports the answer?

options.text: "Theo's kite was stuck high in the tree, and he could not reach it."
options.rationale: This sentence directly explains why Theo was upset.
This detail does not explain why Theo was upset.

answer: A. "Theo's kite was stuck high in the tree, and he could not reach it."
explanation: This sentence is the proof because it tells exactly why Theo felt upset.

prompt: You answer, "The dog was scared of the storm." Which choice is the BEST way to support your answer with evidence?

options.text: The text says, "The dog hid under the bed when the thunder boomed."
options.rationale: It uses an exact detail from the text that proves the dog was scared.

answer: B. The text says, "The dog hid under the bed when the thunder boomed."
explanation: Good support uses an exact detail from the text. The dog hiding during thunder proves it was scared.

lessons.subject: rla
lessons.title: Retell, Paraphrase, and Summarize
lessons.intro: After you read, you can tell what happened in your own words. In this lesson you will learn three ways to do that: retell, paraphrase, and summarize.

vocabulary.meaning: To tell the events of a text in order, in your own words.
To say one part of a text in your own words.
To tell only the most important parts in a short way.

body.text: Three ways to share a text
Each way uses your own words, but they are a little different. A retell keeps the order of events. A paraphrase restates one part. A summary keeps only the big ideas.

body.html:
SkillWhat you do
RetellTell what happened first, next, and last
ParaphraseRestate one part in your own words
SummarizeGive only the most important ideas, short and clear

body.text: Steps to summarize

items: Name the topic or main character.
Tell the most important thing that happened.
Leave out small details.
Keep it short.

body.title: Remember
body.text: A summary is short. If you include every detail, it becomes a retell instead.

prompt: A long story tells how Nina was nervous on her first day, met a friend at recess, and felt happy by the end. Which is the BEST summary?

options.text: Nina wore a blue backpack and ate a sandwich.
options.rationale: These are small details, not the most important ideas.

options.text: Nina was nervous on her first day, but she made a friend and felt happy.
options.rationale: It keeps only the most important ideas in a short way.

options.text: Nina woke up, brushed her teeth, packed her bag, walked to the bus, sat down, looked out the window, and arrived.

answer: B. Nina was nervous on her first day, but she made a friend and felt happy.
explanation: A good summary keeps only the big ideas and stays short, which choice B does.

prompt: The text says, "The enormous, ancient oak tree towered over the tiny playground." Which is the best paraphrase?

options.text: The big, old oak tree stood high above the small playground.
options.rationale: It restates the same idea using different words.

options.text: The playground had swings and slides.
options.rationale: This adds new ideas that were not in the sentence.

options.text: The enormous, ancient oak tree towered over the tiny playground.
options.rationale: This copies the words exactly instead of using your own words.

answer: A. The big, old oak tree stood high above the small playground.
explanation: A paraphrase keeps the same meaning but uses your own words, like changing "enormous, ancient" to "big, old."

lessons.subject: rla
lessons.title: Evaluating Details to Find Key Ideas
lessons.intro: Not every detail is equally important. In this lesson you will learn to sort big, important details from small ones so you can find the key idea of a text.

vocabulary.meaning: A small piece of information in a text.
An important point the details help explain.
To decide how important something is.

body.text: Big details and small details
Some details are important because they help explain the key idea. Other details are interesting but small. A strong reader can tell them apart.
How to evaluate a detail
Ask, "Does this detail help explain the key idea, or is it just extra?" If the key idea would change without the detail, then the detail is important.

items: Important: helps explain the key idea.
Less important: a small fact that is nice to know but not needed.

body.title: Tip
body.text: A key idea is supported by several important details working together.

prompt: A text explains that recycling saves trees, saves energy, and keeps trash out of the ground. Which detail is MOST important to the key idea that recycling helps the Earth?

options.rationale: This is a small detail and does not explain how recycling helps the Earth.
This detail directly explains how recycling helps the Earth.

answer: B. Recycling saves trees and energy
explanation: This detail explains the key idea that recycling helps the Earth, so it is the most important.

prompt: The key idea is that penguins stay warm in the cold. Which detail does NOT help explain this key idea?

options.text: Penguins have thick layers of fat
options.rationale: This helps explain how they stay warm.
This also helps explain how they stay warm.
Their color does not explain how they stay warm, so it is not important to the key idea.

answer: C. Penguins are black and white
explanation: Color does not help explain how penguins stay warm, so it does not support the key idea.

lessons.subject: rla
lessons.title: How Characters Interact and Change
lessons.intro: Characters in a story talk, help, argue, and learn from each other. In this lesson you will describe how characters interact and how they change from the beginning to the end.

vocabulary.meaning: A person or animal in a story.
What happens when characters talk or act with each other.
How a character is different at the end than at the start.

body.text: Characters work together
When characters interact, they affect each other. One character might help another, teach a lesson, or cause a problem. Watch what characters say and do to each other.
Characters change
Many characters are different by the end of a story. They might learn something, become braver, or be kinder. Compare how a character feels and acts at the beginning and at the end.

body.html: Beginning: How is the character? → End: How are they different?

body.title: Tip
body.text: Ask what event made the character change. Usually something happens that teaches them a lesson.

prompt: At the start, Liam refuses to share his blocks. After his friend shares crayons with him, Liam smiles and shares his blocks too. How does Liam change?

options.rationale: At the start he would not share, but by the end he does, so he changed by learning to share.

answer: B. He learns to share
explanation: Liam would not share at first, but after his friend shared, he did too. That shows he changed by learning to share.

prompt: The text says, "At first Mia laughed at the new student, but when the student helped her find her lost dog, Mia said sorry and they became friends." What caused Mia to change?

options.text: The new student helped Mia find her dog
options.rationale: This kind act is the event that made Mia change her feelings.

answer: A. The new student helped Mia find her dog
explanation: The student's kind act of helping find the dog is what made Mia change from laughing to being friends.

lessons.subject: rla
lessons.title: Figurative Language: Similes and More
lessons.intro: Authors use special words to paint pictures in your mind. In this lesson you will spot figurative language, especially the simile, and explain what it really means.

vocabulary.meaning: Words that mean more than their plain meaning to create a picture.
A comparison of two things using the word like or as.
A comparison that says one thing is another, without using like or as.

body.text: What is a simile?
A simile compares two different things using the word like or as. It helps you picture something in a fun way. "Her smile was as bright as the sun" compares a smile to the sun.

body.html: Simile clue words: like and as. Example: "He ran like the wind" means he ran very fast.

body.text: Explaining the meaning
When you find a simile, ask what the two things have in common. "As busy as a bee" means very busy, because bees are always working.

body.title: Remember
body.text: A simile does not mean the words are really true. "Quiet as a mouse" does not mean the person is a mouse—just very quiet.

prompt: Which sentence contains a simile?

options.text: The puppy was small and brown.
options.rationale: This describes the puppy but does not compare it using like or as.

options.text: The puppy was as soft as a cloud.
options.rationale: It compares the puppy to a cloud using the word as, which makes it a simile.

options.text: The puppy ran into the yard.

answer: B. The puppy was as soft as a cloud.
explanation: A simile uses like or as to compare two things. This sentence uses "as soft as a cloud."

prompt: What does the simile "The classroom was as loud as a stadium" mean?

options.rationale: Comparing the room to a loud stadium shows it was very noisy.

options.text: The classroom was a real stadium

answer: B. The classroom was very noisy
explanation: Stadiums are very loud, so comparing the classroom to one means the classroom was very noisy.

lessons.subject: rla
lessons.title: Plot Elements and the Story's Theme
lessons.intro: Every story has parts that fit together, and a lesson the author wants you to learn. In this lesson you will name the plot elements and figure out the theme.

vocabulary.meaning: The events that happen in a story, in order.
The trouble or challenge the characters face.
How the problem gets fixed.
The lesson or big message of the story.

body.text: The parts of a plot
A plot usually has a beginning where you meet the characters, a middle where a problem grows, and an end where the problem is solved.

body.alt: A plot line showing beginning, problem, and solution
body.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 300 110' role='img'><polyline points='20,90 110,90 200,25 280,80' fill='none' stroke='#2563eb' stroke-width='3'/><circle cx='20' cy='90' r='5' fill='#2563eb'/><circle cx='200' cy='25' r='5' fill='#dc2626'/><circle cx='280' cy='80' r='5' fill='#16a34a'/><text x='20' y='105' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Beginning</text><text x='200' y='15' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle' fill='#dc2626'>Problem</text><text x='275' y='98' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle' fill='#16a34a'>Solution</text></svg>

body.text: Finding the theme
The theme is the lesson the story teaches, like "never give up" or "be kind to others." Look at how the problem is solved and what the characters learn.

body.title: Tip
body.text: The theme is usually a lesson about life, not just one thing that happened in the story.

prompt: In a story, a turtle keeps trying to climb a hill even after falling many times, and finally reaches the top. What is the problem in the plot?

options.text: The turtle is having trouble climbing the hill
options.rationale: The challenge the turtle faces is the problem of the plot.

options.text: The hill is made of grass

answer: A. The turtle is having trouble climbing the hill
explanation: The problem is the challenge the character faces, which is the turtle struggling to climb the hill.

prompt: The turtle falls many times but keeps trying and finally reaches the top. What is the theme of this story?

options.rationale: This is a fact, not the lesson the story teaches.

options.text: Never give up, even when something is hard
options.rationale: The turtle keeps trying until it succeeds, which teaches this lesson.

answer: B. Never give up, even when something is hard
explanation: The turtle keeps trying after falling, so the lesson, or theme, is to never give up.
